title: A Panel on the Right to Be Forgotten
summary: Two panellists debate whether individuals should have the legal right to have personal information removed from internet search results, exploring the tension between privacy, free expression, and the public's right to information.

body.speaker: Panellist A
body.text: The right to be forgotten is, at its core, about whether individuals should have meaningful control over their own digital identity. If someone made a mistake twenty years ago — a minor conviction, a photograph taken out of context, an article written in anger — why should that follow them indefinitely in a way that has no equivalent in pre-digital life?

body.speaker: Panellist B
body.text: Because the internet didn't create the information — it merely made existing information more accessible. The conviction existed on public record before Google. The article was published before search engines. What you're really arguing for is not the right to be forgotten but the right to be harder to find. And that's a different and more troubling claim.

body.speaker: Panellist A
body.text: Accessibility isn't a neutral property. There's a meaningful difference between information that is technically on public record and information that appears on the first page of a search result when someone types your name. The practical effect on a person's life, their employment, their relationships, is entirely different. Ignoring that difference isn't principled — it's convenient.

body.speaker: Panellist B
body.text: But who decides what is 'accessible enough'? You're proposing a mechanism by which individuals can petition to have information about themselves hidden from search results. That mechanism will inevitably be used not by people who made genuine mistakes but by the powerful — politicians, executives, organisations — who want to suppress legitimate scrutiny.

body.speaker: Panellist A
body.text: That's a real risk, and it's why any right to be forgotten has to be bounded by a public interest test. Information that relates to the exercise of public power — how a politician voted, how a company behaved — should be exempt from removal requests. The right is for private individuals in relation to genuinely private information.

body.speaker: Panellist B
body.text: But who enforces that distinction? In practice, the boundary between public figure and private individual is contested. A local councillor who embezzled money might claim they are now a private person. A company director convicted of fraud might argue the conviction was twenty years ago. The public interest test sounds coherent in principle but is extremely difficult to apply consistently.

body.speaker: Panellist A
body.text: The difficulty of application is not a reason to abandon the principle. Every legal right is difficult to apply at the margins — that's not unique to this area. What matters is whether the principle itself is sound. And I'd argue it is: a person's digital history should not be permanent by default.

body.speaker: Panellist B
body.text: I'd argue the opposite: information about people should be findable by default, and the burden should be on those who want to conceal it to demonstrate a compelling private interest. The alternative — where information is suppressed unless there is a compelling public interest in its disclosure — creates an information ecosystem that defaults to opacity rather than transparency.

body.speaker: Panellist A
body.text: That framing assumes that all information in public circulation belongs to the public. But information about a person — even if technically public — is theirs in some morally meaningful sense. The right to privacy is not just about secrets. It's about the extent to which others can shape your identity without your participation.

body.speaker: Panellist B
body.text: And the right to free expression is not just about secrets either. It includes the right of historians, journalists, and ordinary people to remember and record. A world in which memories can be selectively erased at individual request is a world with a fundamentally compromised public record.

body.speaker: Panellist A
body.text: Search results are not the public record. A paper archive is. The distinction matters enormously. Making information harder to find through a search engine does not erase it from the public record — it merely adjusts its prominence. If historians need it, it remains accessible. What changes is its immediate, perpetual availability to everyone with an internet connection.

body.speaker: Panellist B
body.text: That's a fair distinction and probably the strongest argument for your position. I'll concede that the permanence of search indexing does create a qualitatively different situation from paper archives. But I remain unconvinced that the solution is individual control over search results. The better path is smarter indexing, temporal weighting, and algorithmic design — not legal rights of suppression.

annotations.meaningVi: quyền được xoá thông tin
annotations.exampleEn: The European Court of Justice established a legal right to be forgotten in 2014.

annotations.meaningVi: danh tính số
annotations.exampleEn: Search results now form a significant part of a person's digital identity.

annotations.meaningVi: bị lấy ra khỏi ngữ cảnh
annotations.exampleEn: A photograph taken out of context can seriously damage someone's reputation.

annotations.meaningVi: khả năng tiếp cận không phải là thuộc tính trung lập
annotations.exampleEn: Making information more accessible changes its social impact — accessibility isn't a neutral property.

annotations.meaningVi: kiểm tra lợi ích công cộng
annotations.exampleEn: A public interest test determines whether the right to information outweighs the right to privacy.

annotations.meaningVi: được miễn khỏi yêu cầu xoá
annotations.exampleEn: Information about the exercise of public power should be exempt from removal requests.

annotations.meaningVi: tham ô; biển thủ
annotations.exampleEn: A local official who embezzled public funds cannot claim immunity from scrutiny by becoming a private citizen.

annotations.meaningVi: khó áp dụng một cách nhất quán
annotations.exampleEn: The public interest test sounds clear in principle but is difficult to apply consistently in practice.

annotations.meaningVi: ở ranh giới; trong các trường hợp cực đoan
annotations.exampleEn: Every legal right is difficult to apply at the margins — edge cases test every principle.

annotations.meaningVi: vĩnh viễn theo mặc định
annotations.exampleEn: Digital information should not be permanent by default — people change and circumstances evolve.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể tìm thấy theo mặc định
annotations.exampleEn: If information about public events is findable by default, accountability is easier to maintain.

annotations.meaningVi: gánh nặng chứng minh
annotations.exampleEn: The burden should be on those who want to conceal information, not on those who seek it.

annotations.meaningVi: sự mờ đục thay vì minh bạch
annotations.exampleEn: Defaulting to suppression creates an information ecosystem of opacity rather than transparency.

annotations.meaningVi: đang lưu hành công khai
annotations.exampleEn: Just because information is in public circulation does not mean the subject has no privacy interest in it.

annotations.phrase: shape your identity without your participation
annotations.meaningVi: định hình danh tính của bạn mà không có sự tham gia của bạn
annotations.exampleEn: Others can shape your identity without your participation through search results you cannot control.

annotations.meaningVi: bị xoá một cách có chọn lọc
annotations.exampleEn: A history that can be selectively erased at individual request is a compromised history.

annotations.meaningVi: hồ sơ công cộng bị tổn hại
annotations.exampleEn: If information can be removed on request, the public record becomes a compromised one.

annotations.meaningVi: điều chỉnh mức độ nổi bật của nó
annotations.exampleEn: De-indexing a result adjusts its prominence without erasing it from the public record.

annotations.meaningVi: điều chỉnh theo thời gian
annotations.exampleEn: Temporal weighting could give older information less visibility in search results without deletion.

annotations.meaningVi: thiết kế thuật toán
annotations.exampleEn: Algorithmic design choices determine what information is most visible to users.

grammarNotes.title: Concessive framing: 'That's a real risk... and it's why...'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi một người phản bác bằng cách thừa nhận trước điểm yếu của lập luận mình, sau đó chuyển hướng, đây là kỹ thuật tu từ quan trọng trong tranh luận. 'That's a real risk, and it's why any right to be forgotten has to be bounded by a public interest test' — câu đầu thừa nhận, câu sau đề xuất giải pháp. Cấu trúc 'That's X, and it's why Y' liên kết thừa nhận với giải pháp một cách tự nhiên. Tương tự: 'The difficulty of application is not a reason to abandon the principle' — phủ nhận kết luận không mong muốn từ một sự thật đã thừa nhận.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Concessive acknowledgement followed by redirection is central to formal debate. 'That's a real risk, and it's why any right to be forgotten has to be bounded by a public interest test' concedes the risk and immediately converts it into justification for a condition. 'The difficulty of application is not a reason to abandon the principle' denies a conclusion from an admitted fact — a classic rebuttal structure. Both are indispensable in B2–C1 level argumentation.

grammarNotes.title: Passive constructions with policy language
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong diễn ngôn chính sách và pháp lý, cấu trúc bị động thường được dùng để tập trung vào hành động hơn là tác nhân thực hiện: 'information should be exempt from removal requests' (không nói ai miễn trừ), 'a world in which memories can be selectively erased' (không nói ai xoá). Bị động với 'bounded by', 'exempted from', 'suppressed unless' đều là các cụm từ quan trọng trong văn bản pháp lý và học thuật về quyền dữ liệu ở trình độ B2–C1.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Policy discourse frequently uses passive constructions to focus on the action or condition rather than the agent: 'information should be exempt from removal requests', 'memories can be selectively erased', 'the burden should be on those who want to conceal it'. These passives allow discussion of systemic effects without specifying who is acting. Recognising passive voice in complex policy arguments is a key B2 reading and listening skill.

questions.prompt: How does Panellist A define the core issue of the right to be forgotten?
options: Whether the internet should be regulated by governments
Whether individuals should have meaningful control over their own digital identity
Whether search engines should be allowed to collect personal data
Whether historical records should be digitised at all
questions.explanation: Panellist A opens: 'The right to be forgotten is, at its core, about whether individuals should have meaningful control over their own digital identity.'
questions.hint: Listen for the phrase 'at its core' and what follows it.

questions.prompt: What does Panellist B say the internet actually did, rather than creating new information?
options: It created entirely new types of information that did not exist before
It made existing information more accessible
It allowed people to create false information about others
It permanently erased older information from paper records
questions.explanation: Panellist B says 'the internet didn't create the information — it merely made existing information more accessible.'
questions.hint: Listen for Panellist B's distinction between creation and accessibility.

questions.prompt: What does Panellist B say Panellist A is actually arguing for, rather than the 'right to be forgotten'?
options: The right to be anonymous online
The right to delete all internet data permanently
The right to be harder to find
The right to control what search engines display
questions.explanation: Panellist B says 'What you're really arguing for is not the right to be forgotten but the right to be harder to find. And that's a different and more troubling claim.'
questions.hint: Listen for Panellist B's reframing of Panellist A's position.

questions.prompt: Why does Panellist A say the difference in accessibility is not a neutral matter?
options: Because some information is more accurate than others
Because accessible information has a fundamentally different practical effect on a person's life, employment, and relationships
Because accessibility depends on who is searching, not just what is being searched
Because neutral accessibility would require equal treatment of all information
questions.explanation: Panellist A argues 'there's a meaningful difference between information that is technically on public record and information that appears on the first page of a search result' — the 'practical effect on a person's life, their employment, their relationships, is entirely different.'
questions.hint: Listen for the three areas of life Panellist A says are affected by accessibility.

questions.prompt: Who does Panellist B fear will actually use the right to be forgotten?
options: Ordinary people with minor criminal convictions
Young people embarrassed by social media posts
The powerful — politicians, executives, and organisations — who want to suppress legitimate scrutiny
Victims of online harassment seeking to protect themselves
questions.explanation: Panellist B says the mechanism 'will inevitably be used not by people who made genuine mistakes but by the powerful — politicians, executives, organisations — who want to suppress legitimate scrutiny.'
questions.hint: Listen for who Panellist B identifies as the likely users of the right.

questions.prompt: What safeguard does Panellist A propose to prevent abuse of the right to be forgotten?
options: A time limit of five years on any removal
Judicial review of all removal requests
A public interest test, with exemptions for information about the exercise of public power
An independent ombudsman to review disputed cases
questions.explanation: Panellist A says 'any right to be forgotten has to be bounded by a public interest test. Information that relates to the exercise of public power... should be exempt from removal requests.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific test Panellist A proposes.

questions.prompt: What practical example does Panellist B give to show the difficulty of the public/private boundary?
options: A journalist who wrote an inaccurate story claiming to be a private person
A local councillor who embezzled money claiming they are now a private person
A celebrity seeking to remove embarrassing photographs
A corporation trying to remove negative reviews
questions.explanation: Panellist B says 'A local councillor who embezzled money might claim they are now a private person.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific professional title and crime Panellist B mentions.

questions.prompt: How does Panellist A respond to the argument that application is difficult?
options: By agreeing that the difficulty makes the right unworkable
By arguing that difficulty of application is not a reason to abandon a sound principle
By proposing simpler criteria for the public interest test
By saying courts are better placed to handle these cases than regulators
questions.explanation: Panellist A says 'The difficulty of application is not a reason to abandon the principle. Every legal right is difficult to apply at the margins.'
questions.hint: Listen for Panellist A's statement about what the difficulty does and does not justify.

questions.prompt: What default does Panellist B argue information should have?
options: Information should be private by default unless the subject consents to publication
Information should be findable by default, with the burden on those who want to conceal it
Information should be removed after five years by default
Information should be neutral by default, with neither privacy nor accessibility assumed
questions.explanation: Panellist B says 'information about people should be findable by default, and the burden should be on those who want to conceal it to demonstrate a compelling private interest.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific default Panellist B proposes and where they say the burden should lie.

questions.prompt: What concern does Panellist B raise about Panellist B's alternative system?
options: It would create an information ecosystem that defaults to transparency rather than opacity
It would create an information ecosystem that defaults to opacity rather than transparency
It would be too expensive for small search engines to implement
It would require all information to be manually reviewed by regulators
questions.explanation: Panellist B says the alternative 'creates an information ecosystem that defaults to opacity rather than transparency.'
questions.hint: Listen for the 'opacity versus transparency' contrast.

questions.prompt: How does Panellist A define what the right to privacy is 'not just about'?
options: Privacy is not just about secrets — it is about the extent to which others can shape your identity without your participation
Privacy is not just about personal data — it is about freedom from all surveillance
Privacy is not just about legal rights — it is about social norms
Privacy is not just about individuals — it is about collective identity
questions.explanation: Panellist A says 'The right to privacy is not just about secrets. It's about the extent to which others can shape your identity without your participation.'
questions.hint: Listen for Panellist A's extended definition of what privacy is about.

questions.prompt: What is Panellist A's key distinction between search results and the public record?
options: Search results are more accurate than paper archives
Search results are not the public record — a paper archive is; removing from search results only adjusts prominence, not existence
Search results are regulated differently from paper archives in law
Search results are temporary while paper archives are permanent
questions.explanation: Panellist A says 'Search results are not the public record. A paper archive is... Making information harder to find through a search engine does not erase it from the public record — it merely adjusts its prominence.'
questions.hint: Listen for the key distinction Panellist A draws in the final exchanges.

questions.prompt: What does Panellist B concede at the end of the debate?
options: That the right to be forgotten should be implemented immediately
That search results and paper archives are equivalent
That the permanence of search indexing creates a qualitatively different situation from paper archives
That individual control over search results is the best solution available
questions.explanation: Panellist B says 'I'll concede that the permanence of search indexing does create a qualitatively different situation from paper archives.'
questions.hint: Listen for the explicit concession Panellist B makes in their final turn.

questions.prompt: What does Panellist B propose as an alternative to individual legal rights of suppression?
options: Government-mandated deletion of all information older than ten years
Smarter indexing, temporal weighting, and algorithmic design
A global database of protected individuals
A universal right to privacy enforced by international courts
questions.explanation: Panellist B says 'The better path is smarter indexing, temporal weighting, and algorithmic design — not legal rights of suppression.'
questions.hint: Listen for the three technical solutions Panellist B proposes as alternatives.

questions.prompt: What does 'temporal weighting' mean in the context of search results?
options: Measuring how long a page has been indexed
Giving older information lower prominence in search results over time
Weighting search results by the time of day they are accessed
Removing information after a fixed period of time
questions.explanation: In context, 'temporal weighting' is offered as an alternative to deletion — making older information less prominent over time rather than requiring legal removal.
questions.hint: Think about what 'temporal' means (relating to time) and what 'weighting' does to prominence.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the overall structure of this debate?
options: Both panellists agree from the start and simply discuss implementation
Panellist A makes only one argument and Panellist B demolishes it
Each panellist advances principled arguments, concedes some points, and maintains a core position by the end
The panellists argue past each other without engaging with the other's points
questions.explanation: Both panellists acknowledge strengths in the other's position (Panellist A concedes the risk of abuse; Panellist B concedes the archive/search distinction) while maintaining their core positions.
questions.hint: Think about what each panellist agrees with and what they still disagree about at the end.

cloze.template:
Panellist A: The right to be forgotten is, at its core, about whether individuals should have meaningful control over their own {{b1}} identity. If someone made a mistake twenty years ago — a minor conviction, a photograph taken out of context, an article written in anger — why should that follow them indefinitely in a way that has no equivalent in pre-digital life?

Panellist B: Because the internet didn't create the information — it merely made existing information more {{b2}}. The conviction existed on public record before Google. The article was published before search engines. What you're really arguing for is not the right to be forgotten but the right to be harder to {{b3}}. And that's a different and more troubling claim.

Panellist A: Accessibility isn't a neutral property. There's a meaningful difference between information that is technically on public record and information that appears on the first page of a search result when someone types your name. The practical effect on a person's life, their employment, their relationships, is entirely different. Ignoring that difference isn't principled — it's {{b4}}.

Panellist B: But who decides what is 'accessible enough'? You're proposing a mechanism by which individuals can petition to have information about themselves hidden from search results. That mechanism will inevitably be used not by people who made genuine mistakes but by the powerful — politicians, executives, organisations — who want to suppress legitimate {{b5}}.

Panellist A: That's a real risk, and it's why any right to be forgotten has to be bounded by a {{b6}} test. Information that relates to the exercise of public power — how a politician voted, how a company behaved — should be exempt from removal requests. The right is for private individuals in relation to genuinely private information.

Panellist B: But who enforces that distinction? In practice, the boundary between public figure and private individual is {{b7}}. A local councillor who embezzled money might claim they are now a private person. The public interest test sounds coherent in principle but is extremely difficult to apply consistently.

Panellist A: The difficulty of application is not a reason to abandon the principle. Every legal right is difficult to apply at the {{b8}} — that's not unique to this area. What matters is whether the principle itself is sound. And I'd argue it is: a person's digital history should not be permanent by default.

Panellist B: I'd argue the opposite: information about people should be findable by default, and the burden should be on those who want to conceal it to demonstrate a compelling private interest. The alternative creates an information ecosystem that defaults to {{b9}} rather than transparency.

Panellist A: That framing assumes that all information in public circulation belongs to the public. But information about a person — even if technically public — is theirs in some morally meaningful sense. The right to privacy is not just about secrets. It's about the extent to which others can shape your identity without your {{b10}}.

Panellist B: And the right to free expression is not just about secrets either. It includes the right of historians, journalists, and ordinary people to remember and record. A world in which memories can be selectively erased at individual request is a world with a fundamentally {{b11}} public record.

Panellist A: Search results are not the public record. A paper archive is. The distinction matters enormously. Making information harder to find through a search engine does not erase it from the public record — it merely adjusts its {{b12}}. If historians need it, it remains accessible.

Panellist B: That's a fair distinction and probably the strongest argument for your position. I'll concede that the permanence of search indexing does create a qualitatively different situation from paper archives. But I remain unconvinced that the solution is individual control over search results. The better path is smarter indexing, temporal {{b13}}, and algorithmic design — not legal rights of suppression.

options: digital
personal
online
public
blanks.explanation: 'Digital identity' — the way a person is represented and found online.

options: accessible
available
visible
searchable
blanks.explanation: The internet made information 'more accessible' — easier to find, not new information.

options: find
forget
hide
remove
blanks.explanation: Panellist B reframes: the right is really 'to be harder to find', not to be forgotten.

options: convenient
honest
accurate
neutral
blanks.explanation: Ignoring the difference 'isn't principled — it's convenient' — a dismissal without intellectual honesty.

options: scrutiny
criticism
reporting
accountability
blanks.explanation: The powerful want to suppress 'legitimate scrutiny' — proper examination of their conduct.

options: public interest
privacy
judicial
proportionality
blanks.explanation: A 'public interest test' determines whether information relates to the exercise of public power.

options: contested
unclear
disputed
blurred
blanks.explanation: 'Contested' — genuinely disputed, not clearly settled in all cases.

options: margins
edges
limits
extremes
blanks.explanation: 'At the margins' — in edge cases where the rule is hardest to apply.

options: opacity
secrecy
privacy
restriction
blanks.explanation: 'Opacity' — lack of transparency; hiding rather than revealing.

options: participation
consent
knowledge
permission
blanks.explanation: Others shape your identity 'without your participation' — you have no say in how you are presented.

options: compromised
damaged
weakened
corrupted
blanks.explanation: A 'fundamentally compromised public record' — one that cannot be trusted as complete or accurate.

options: prominence
visibility
ranking
position
blanks.explanation: Adjusting 'prominence' — how visible and easily found something is, not whether it exists.

options: weighting
filtering
ranking
sorting
blanks.explanation: 'Temporal weighting' — giving older information progressively less prominence over time.

translationVi: Hai thành viên hội thảo tranh luận về quyền được xoá thông tin khỏi kết quả tìm kiếm. Thành viên A lập luận rằng đây là vấn đề kiểm soát danh tính số — tại sao lỗi lầm từ 20 năm trước lại phải theo đuổi ai đó mãi mãi? Thành viên B phản bác rằng internet không tạo ra thông tin mà chỉ làm nó dễ tiếp cận hơn, và lập luận thực sự là 'quyền khó tìm thấy hơn'. Thành viên A lập luận rằng khả năng tiếp cận không phải là thuộc tính trung lập — việc xuất hiện ở trang đầu kết quả tìm kiếm có tác động thực tế hoàn toàn khác. Thành viên B lo ngại rằng quyền này sẽ bị người có quyền lực lạm dụng. Thành viên A đề xuất kiểm tra lợi ích công cộng. Hai bên tranh luận về mặc định nào — có thể tìm thấy hay riêng tư — phù hợp hơn. Thành viên A đưa ra điểm mấu chốt rằng kết quả tìm kiếm không phải là hồ sơ công cộng, và việc xoá khỏi tìm kiếm chỉ điều chỉnh mức độ nổi bật, không xoá khỏi hồ sơ. Thành viên B nhượng bộ điểm này nhưng đề xuất thiết kế thuật toán thay vì quyền pháp lý.
criticalThinkingQuestion: Panellist A argues that the right to privacy includes 'the extent to which others can shape your identity without your participation'. Panellist B responds that free expression includes 'the right of historians, journalists, and ordinary people to remember and record'. Both rights are fundamental. In your view, which value should set the default — privacy or findability — and what would be lost by choosing one over the other? Is there a way to design systems that genuinely protect both without requiring case-by-case legal adjudication?
